prompt:
I do backup verification for our media archive and I keep hand-rolling `find | xargs sha256sum` pipelines that fall over on symlinks and print in whatever order the disk feels like. I want a proper little library I can build the manifest CLI on top of. Module go-treehash, package treehash, in this repo.

What I need: HashTree(root string, opts Options) ([]Entry, error) that walks root recursively and returns one Entry per regular file: Path (relative to root, forward slashes), Size in bytes, SHA256 (lowercase hex). The slice comes back sorted by Path as a plain byte-wise string sort — careful, that means "a.txt" sorts before "a/one.txt" — and the output must be byte-identical run to run no matter how many workers are hashing.

Options:
- Workers: max number of files being hashed at the same time; anything < 1 behaves like 1
- MinSize / MaxSize: keep only files with MinSize <= size, and size <= MaxSize when MaxSize > 0 (both bounds inclusive; MaxSize 0 = no upper bound, MinSize 0 keeps empty files)
- Progress func(Event), Event{Path string; Done bool}: if non-nil, the hashing worker calls it synchronously right before it starts a file (Done=false) and right after it finishes (Done=true). Exactly one start/done pair per hashed file, and only for files that pass the filters. This is how the CLI will draw its progress bar, and it's also how the tests watch that you actually honor the Workers cap instead of blasting everything at once.

Symlinks: never follow, never report — file symlinks, directory symlinks, broken ones: all invisible, and a symlinked directory's contents must not get pulled in. Empty tree (or all filtered out) → empty result, nil error. Root doesn't exist → error. A file that can't be opened/read → return an error that mentions the path.

treehash_test.go is the acceptance contract — hands off. go test -race -timeout 30s ./... must pass. Please keep walking, hashing, and the public API in separate source files; last tool like this I inherited was one 600-line main.go and I'm still in therapy.
